context.pr_body:
> Changed in Python version 3.12: Unrecognized escape sequences produce a SyntaxWarning. In a future Python version they will be eventually a SyntaxError.
[2.4.1.1. Escape sequences](https://docs.python.org/3/reference/lexical_analysis.html#index-23)

Change to raw string literal to fix:
```
/webserver.py:17: SyntaxWarning: invalid escape sequence '\d'
  elif re.match("/status/(\d+)", self.path):
/webserver.py:18: SyntaxWarning: invalid escape sequence '\d'
  result = re.match("/status/(\d+)", self.path)
```

agents.name: analysis_agent
agents.system_prompt:
You are an Analysis Agent specialized in Pull Request analysis.

## Project Context (MASCA Analysis)
## Project Analysis

### README
[![Test](https://github.com/nginx-proxy/nginx-proxy/actions/workflows/test.yml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/nginx-proxy/nginx-proxy/actions/workflows/test.yml)
[![GitHub release](https://img.shields.io/github/v/release/nginx-proxy/nginx-proxy)](https://github.com/nginx-proxy/nginx-proxy/releases)
![nginx 1.26.0](https://img.shields.io/badge/nginx-1.26.0-brightgreen.svg)
[![Docker Image Size](https://img.shields.io/docker/image-size/nginxproxy/nginx-proxy?sort=semver)](https://hub.docker.com/r/nginxproxy/nginx-proxy "Click to view the image on Docker Hub")
[![Docker stars](https://img.shields.io/docker/stars/nginxproxy/nginx-proxy.svg)](https://hub.docker.com/r/nginxproxy/nginx-proxy "DockerHub")
[![Docker pulls](https://img.shields.io/docker/pulls/nginxproxy/nginx-proxy.svg)](https://hub.docker.com/r/nginxproxy/nginx-proxy "DockerHub")

nginx-proxy sets up a container running nginx and [docker-gen](https://github.com/nginx-proxy/docker-gen). docker-gen generates reverse proxy configs for nginx and reloads nginx when containers are started and stopped.

See [Automated Nginx Reverse Proxy for Docker](http://jasonwilder.com/blog/2014/03/25/automated-nginx-reverse-proxy-for-docker/) for why you might want to use this.

### Usage

To run it:

```console
docker run --detach \
    --name nginx-proxy \
    --publish 80:80 \
    --volume /var/run/docker.sock:/tmp/docker.sock:ro \
    nginxproxy/nginx-proxy:1.5
```

Then start any containers (here an nginx container) you want proxied with an env var `VIRTUAL_HOST=subdomain.yourdomain.com`

```console
docker run --detach \
    --name your-proxied-app \
    --env VIRTUAL_HOST=foo.bar.com \
    nginx
```

Provided your DNS is setup to resolve `foo.bar.com` to the host running nginx-proxy, a request to `http://foo.bar.com` will then be routed to a container with the `VIRTUAL_HOST` env var set to `foo.bar.com` (in this case, the **your-proxied-app** container).

The containers being proxied must :

- [expose](https://docs.docker.com/engine/reference/run/#expose-incoming-ports) the port to be proxied, either by using the `EXPOSE` directive in their `Dockerfile` or by using the `--expose` flag to `docker run` or `docker create`.
- share at least one Docker network with the nginx-proxy container: by default, if you don't pass the `--net` flag when your nginx-proxy container is created, it will only be attached to the default bridge network. This means that it will not be able to connect to containers on networks other than bridge.

Note: providing a port number in `VIRTUAL_HOST` isn't suported, please see [virtual ports](https://github.com/nginx-proxy/nginx-proxy/tree/main/docs#virtual-ports) or [custom external HTTP/HTTPS ports](https://github.com/nginx-proxy/nginx-proxy/tree/main/docs#custom-external-httphttps-ports) depending on what you want to achieve.

### Image variants

The nginx-proxy images are available in two flavors.

#### Debian based version

This image is based on the nginx:mainline image, itself


## Your Task
When you receive a PR title and body, you must:

1. **Analyze the request**: Understand what the PR wants to achieve
2. **Explore the code**: Use tools to navigate base_project and find relevant files/functions
3. **Identify targets**: Determine exactly which files and functions need to be modified
4. **Optimize MASCA**: Produce a version of the MASCA analysis focused on the specific PR

## Available Tools
- `read_base_project_file(file_path)`: Read a file from the project
- `list_base_project_directory(directory, pattern, recursive)`: List files/folders

## Required Output
You must produce a structured output with:
- `pr_title`: PR title
- `pr_body`: PR body
- `masca_optimized`: MASCA optimized for this specific PR
- `files_to_modify`: List of files to modify with motivation
- `functions_to_modify`: List of functions to modify with motivation
- `analysis_summary`: Summary of your analysis

## Guidelines
- Explore the code before drawing conclusions
- Search for files with names relevant to the PR
- Identify specific functions, not just generic files
- Be precise with paths (relative to base_project)
- For function names use the format "ClassName.method_name" for methods
agents.input_prompt:
Analyze this Pull Request:

## Title
fix: tests webserver syntaxwarning

## Description
> Changed in Python version 3.12: Unrecognized escape sequences produce a SyntaxWarning. In a future Python version they will be eventually a SyntaxError.
[2.4.1.1. Escape sequences](https://docs.python.org/3/reference/lexical_analysis.html#index-23)

Change to raw string literal to fix:
```
/webserver.py:17: SyntaxWarning: invalid escape sequence '\d'
  elif re.match("/status/(\d+)", self.path):
/webserver.py:18: SyntaxWarning: invalid escape sequence '\d'
  result = re.match("/status/(\d+)", self.path)
```

Explore the source code to identify the files and functions to modify.

tool_calls.result:
### File Overview
This file implements a tiny HTTP server used for test/requirements/web. It defines a request handler that responds to a few fixed routes ("/headers", "/port", "/status/<code>", "/") and runs a TCP server on PORT from the command line.

### Architecture
- A subclass of http.server.SimpleHTTPRequestHandler named Handler implements routing logic in its do_GET method.
- The module-level main block (if __name__ == '__main__':) reads PORT from sys.argv, configures socketserver.TCPServer, and calls serve_forever() to start the server.
- Routing is implemented with a series of if/elif checks inside do_GET, including two uses of re.match("/status/(\d+)", self.path) to detect the "/status/<code>" route.

### Functions & Classes
- Handler — class
  - Subclass of http.server.SimpleHTTPRequestHandler that provides custom handling for GET requests.
  - Importance: central class handling all incoming HTTP GET requests for this server.

- do_GET — method of Handler
  - Implements the request handling and routing logic.
  - Workflow inside do_GET:
    - Initializes response_body = "" and response_code = 200.
    - Checks routes in order:
      - self.path == "/headers" — appends request headers via self.headers.as_string().
      - self.path == "/port" — appends f"answer from port {PORT}\n".
      - re.match("/status/(\d+)", self.path) — (first occurrence) tests for a "/status/<digits>" route.
      - result = re.match("/status/(\d+)", self.path) — (second occurrence) re-runs the match, then uses result.group(1) to set response_code = int(result.group(1)) and appends f"answer with response code {response_code}\n".
      - self.path == "/" — appends f"I'm {os.environ['HOSTNAME']}\n".
      - else — sets response_body to "No route for this path!\n" and response_code = 404.
    - Sends the response with self.send_response(response_code), self.send_header("Content-Type", "text/plain"), self.end_headers(), and writes the response body via self.wfile.write(response_body.encode()) if non-empty.
  - Importance: the single point to modify to change routing, to fix the regex causing the SyntaxWarning, or to adjust response behavior.

- if __name__ == '__main__': — module-level main block
  - Reads PORT = int(sys.argv[1]).
  - Sets socketserver.TCPServer.allow_reuse_address = True.
  - Creates httpd = socketserver.TCPServer(('0.0.0.0', PORT), Handler) and calls httpd.serve_forever().
  - Importance: starts the server; ensures PORT is available at module-level for do_GET to reference.

- re.match("/status/(\d+)", self.path) — two exact occurrences in do_GET
  - First occurrence used as conditional in an elif: elif re.match("/status/(\d+)", self.path):
  - Second occurrence immediately repeats the match to bind result: result = re.match("/status/(\d+)", self.path)
  - Importance: these lines are the source of the SyntaxWarning (invalid escape sequence in the string "/status/(\d+)") because "\d" is not a valid Python string escape — the pattern should be a raw string (e.g., r"/status/(\d+)") or have the backslash escaped ("\\/status/\\(\\d+\\)" not desirable). Also calling re.match twice is redundant.

### Relevance to Agent's Goal
- The regex causing the SyntaxWarning is the string literal used in the two occurrences of re.match("/status/(\d+)", self.path) inside Handler.do_GET.
- The exact location to modify is Handler.do_GET: change the pattern strings to a raw string (for example, re.match(r"/status/(\d+)", self.path)) and remove the duplicate re.match call (reuse the match result) to both eliminate the SyntaxWarning and avoid redundant matching. Example change points:
  - Replace elif re.match("/status/(\d+)", self.path): with something like:
    - result = re.match(r"/status/(\d+)", self.path)
    - elif result:
      - (use result.group(1) below)
  - Or at minimum change both occurrences to re.match(r"/status/(\d+)", self.path) and keep only one call.
- Any modifications should be made inside the Handler class, specifically the do_GET method, since that's where request routing and response-code extraction occur.

Your summary MUST:
1. Preserve ALL exact function names, class names, and method names exactly as they appear in the source code — never rename or abbreviate them.
2. Explain the overall architecture and purpose of the file.
3. Describe the role and importance of each function, class, and method.
4. Highlight the parts most relevant to the agent's stated goal.

### Architecture

summarization.user_prompt:
## File: test/requirements/web/webserver.py

## Agent's reason for reading this file:
Locate the regex causing SyntaxWarning and see surrounding code to identify function/class to modify

## What the agent expects to find:
Find the lines with re.match("/status/(\d+)", ...) and understand the function handling HTTP requests

## Source Code:
#!/usr/bin/env python3

import os, sys, re
import http.server
import socketserver

class Handler(http.server.SimpleHTTPRequestHandler):
    def do_GET(self):

        response_body = ""
        response_code = 200

        if self.path == "/headers":
            response_body += self.headers.as_string()
        elif self.path == "/port":
            response_body += f"answer from port {PORT}\n"
        elif re.match("/status/(\d+)", self.path):
            result = re.match("/status/(\d+)", self.path)
            response_code = int(result.group(1))
            response_body += f"answer with response code {response_code}\n"
        elif self.path == "/":
            response_body += f"I'm {os.environ['HOSTNAME']}\n"
        else:
            response_body += "No route for this path!\n"
            response_code = 404

        self.send_response(response_code)
        self.send_header("Content-Type", "text/plain")
        self.end_headers()

        if (len(response_body)):
            self.wfile.write(response_body.encode())

if __name__ == '__main__':
    PORT = int(sys.argv[1])
    socketserver.TCPServer.allow_reuse_address = True
    httpd = socketserver.TCPServer(('0.0.0.0', PORT), Handler)
    httpd.serve_forever()

tool_calls.result:
### File Overview
This file implements a tiny HTTP test webserver used in tests: `test/requirements/web/webserver.py`. It defines a request handler class `Handler` (subclassing `http.server.SimpleHTTPRequestHandler`) that responds to a few hard-coded routes and starts a `socketserver.TCPServer` when run as a script.

### Architecture
- Top-level imports: `os`, `sys`, `re`, `http.server`, `socketserver`.
- A request handler class `Handler` overrides `do_GET` to route requests based on `self.path`.
- When executed as a script (inside `if __name__ == '__main__':`), the script reads a `PORT` from `sys.argv[1]`, sets `socketserver.TCPServer.allow_reuse_address = True`, constructs a `socketserver.TCPServer(('0.0.0.0', PORT), Handler)` named `httpd`, and calls `httpd.serve_forever()`.

The Handler builds a plaintext response body and code, sends headers via `self.send_response` / `self.send_header` / `self.end_headers`, and writes the response body via `self.wfile.write()`.

### Functions & Classes
- `Handler` — class that inherits from `http.server.SimpleHTTPRequestHandler`. This is the core request handler for the server; it completely overrides GET handling with `do_GET`. Important because all routing/response behavior is implemented here.

  - `do_GET` — method on `Handler` that handles all incoming GET requests. Behavior details:
    - Initializes `response_body = ""` and `response_code = 200`.
    - Route matching (exact strings and regex):
      - `if self.path == "/headers":` — appends `self.headers.as_string()` to `response_body`. This returns the incoming request headers as a string.
      - `elif self.path == "/port":` — appends `f"answer from port {PORT}\n"` to `response_body`. Note: this references the global `PORT` value defined in the `__main__` block.
      - `elif re.match("/status/(\d+)", self.path):` — performs `re.match("/status/(\d+)", self.path)` (called again immediately) to extract the status code, sets `response_code` to the captured integer, and appends `f"answer with response code {response_code}\n"` to `response_body`. The exact regex string in the code is `"/status/(\d+)"`.
      - `elif self.path == "/":` — appends `f"I'm {os.environ['HOSTNAME']}\n"` to `response_body`. This reads the `HOSTNAME` environment variable directly and will raise a `KeyError` if `HOSTNAME` is not set.
      - `else:` — appends `"No route for this path!\n"` to `response_body` and sets `response_code = 404`.
    - Sends the response: `self.send_response(response_code)`, `self.send_header("Content-Type", "text/plain")`, `self.end_headers()`.
    - Writes the body only if `if (len(response_body)):` then `self.wfile.write(response_body.encode())`.

- `if __name__ == '__main__':` — module-level execution block that runs the server:
  - `PORT = int(sys.argv[1])` — reads the port number from the first command-line argument and assigns the global `PORT`.
  - `socketserver.TCPServer.allow_reuse_address = True` — sets the class attribute to allow immediate reuse of the port.
  - `httpd = socketserver.TCPServer(('0.0.0.0', PORT), Handler)` — creates the TCP server binding to all interfaces and the given `PORT`.
  - `httpd.serve_forever()` — starts serving requests indefinitely.

Other exact names/strings present and relevant:
- Route strings: `"/headers"`, `"/port"`, `"/status/(\d+)"`, `"/"`.
- Response strings exactly as written: `"answer from port {PORT}\n"`, `"answer with response code {response_code}\n"`, `"I'm {os.environ['HOSTNAME']}\n"`, `"No route for this path!\n"`.

summarization.user_prompt:
## File: test/requirements/web/webserver.py

## Agent's reason for reading this file:
Get exact content to reference lines and suggest exact modification

## What the agent expects to find:
Full source to reference exact pattern occurrences and provide suggested code changes

## Source Code:
#!/usr/bin/env python3

import os, sys, re
import http.server
import socketserver

class Handler(http.server.SimpleHTTPRequestHandler):
    def do_GET(self):

        response_body = ""
        response_code = 200

        if self.path == "/headers":
            response_body += self.headers.as_string()
        elif self.path == "/port":
            response_body += f"answer from port {PORT}\n"
        elif re.match("/status/(\d+)", self.path):
            result = re.match("/status/(\d+)", self.path)
            response_code = int(result.group(1))
            response_body += f"answer with response code {response_code}\n"
        elif self.path == "/":
            response_body += f"I'm {os.environ['HOSTNAME']}\n"
        else:
            response_body += "No route for this path!\n"
            response_code = 404

        self.send_response(response_code)
        self.send_header("Content-Type", "text/plain")
        self.end_headers()

        if (len(response_body)):
            self.wfile.write(response_body.encode())

if __name__ == '__main__':
    PORT = int(sys.argv[1])
    socketserver.TCPServer.allow_reuse_address = True
    httpd = socketserver.TCPServer(('0.0.0.0', PORT), Handler)
    httpd.serve_forever()
summarization.summary:
### File Overview
This file implements a tiny HTTP test webserver used in tests: `test/requirements/web/webserver.py`. It defines a request handler class `Handler` (subclassing `http.server.SimpleHTTPRequestHandler`) that responds to a few hard-coded routes and starts a `socketserver.TCPServer` when run as a script.

### Architecture
- Top-level imports: `os`, `sys`, `re`, `http.server`, `socketserver`.
- A request handler class `Handler` overrides `do_GET` to route requests based on `self.path`.
- When executed as a script (inside `if __name__ == '__main__':`), the script reads a `PORT` from `sys.argv[1]`, sets `socketserver.TCPServer.allow_reuse_address = True`, constructs a `socketserver.TCPServer(('0.0.0.0', PORT), Handler)` named `httpd`, and calls `httpd.serve_forever()`.

The Handler builds a plaintext response body and code, sends headers via `self.send_response` / `self.send_header` / `self.end_headers`, and writes the response body via `self.wfile.write()`.

### Functions & Classes
- `Handler` — class that inherits from `http.server.SimpleHTTPRequestHandler`. This is the core request handler for the server; it completely overrides GET handling with `do_GET`. Important because all routing/response behavior is implemented here.

  - `do_GET` — method on `Handler` that handles all incoming GET requests. Behavior details:
    - Initializes `response_body = ""` and `response_code = 200`.
    - Route matching (exact strings and regex):
      - `if self.path == "/headers":` — appends `self.headers.as_string()` to `response_body`. This returns the incoming request headers as a string.
      - `elif self.path == "/port":` — appends `f"answer from port {PORT}\n"` to `response_body`. Note: this references the global `PORT` value defined in the `__main__` block.
      - `elif re.match("/status/(\d+)", self.path):` — performs `re.match("/status/(\d+)", self.path)` (called again immediately) to extract the status code, sets `response_code` to the captured integer, and appends `f"answer with response code {response_code}\n"` to `response_body`. The exact regex string in the code is `"/status/(\d+)"`.
      - `elif self.path == "/":` — appends `f"I'm {os.environ['HOSTNAME']}\n"` to `response_body`. This reads the `HOSTNAME` environment variable directly and will raise a `KeyError` if `HOSTNAME` is not set.
      - `else:` — appends `"No route for this path!\n"` to `response_body` and sets `response_code = 404`.
    - Sends the response: `self.send_response(response_code)`, `self.send_header("Content-Type", "text/plain")`, `self.end_headers()`.
    - Writes the body only if `if (len(response_body)):` then `self.wfile.write(response_body.encode())`.

- `if __name__ == '__main__':` — module-level execution block that runs the server:
  - `PORT = int(sys.argv[1])` — reads the port number from the first command-line argument and assigns the global `PORT`.
  - `socketserver.TCPServer.allow_reuse_address = True` — sets the class attribute to allow immediate reuse of the port.
  - `httpd = socketserver.TCPServer(('0.0.0.0', PORT), Handler)` — creates the TCP server binding to all interfaces and the given `PORT`.
  - `httpd.serve_forever()` — starts serving requests indefinitely.

Other exact names/strings present and relevant:
- Route strings: `"/headers"`, `"/port"`, `"/status/(\d+)"`, `"/"`.
- Response strings exactly as written: `"answer from port {PORT}\n"`, `"answer with response code {response_code}\n"`, `"I'm {os.environ['HOSTNAME']}\n"`, `"No route for this path!\n"`.
- Header setting: `"Content-Type", "text/plain"`.

### Relevance to Agent's Goal
You want exact content to reference lines and suggest exact modifications. Relevant points to reference or change:
- Exact route patterns to update: `"/headers"`, `"/port"`, `"/status/(\d+)"`, `"/"`.
- Exact regex usage: `re.match("/status/(\d+)", self.path)` appears twice in the `do_GET` branch (first as a condition, then re-run to capture groups) — a likely candidate for consolidation.
- Global `PORT` variable usage: `do_GET` references `PORT` (via the `"/port"` path) which is set in the `if __name__ == '__main__':` block — note the dependency on that global variable being defined before requests are handled.
- Environment access: `os.environ['HOSTNAME']` is used directly in `do_GET` and will raise if `HOSTNAME` is absent — a possible improvement point.
- Conditional body write: `if (len(response_body)):` gate before `self.wfile.write(...)` — if the intent is to send empty bodies for some responses, this condition matters.
- Server setup lines to reference for changes: `PORT = int(sys.argv[1])`, `socketserver.TCPServer.allow_reuse_address = True`, `httpd = socketserver.TCPServer(('0.0.0.0', PORT), Handler)`, `httpd.serve_forever()`.

Use the exact names above when proposing changes so suggested replacements or edits can reference the correct symbols and strings.

output.pr_body:
Changed in Python version 3.12: Unrecognized escape sequences produce a SyntaxWarning. In a future Python version they will be eventually a SyntaxError.
[2.4.1.1. Escape sequences](https://docs.python.org/3/reference/lexical_analysis.html#index-23)

Change to raw string literal to fix:
```
/webserver.py:17: SyntaxWarning: invalid escape sequence '\d'
  elif re.match("/status/(\d+)", self.path):
/webserver.py:18: SyntaxWarning: invalid escape sequence '\d'
  result = re.match("/status/(\d+)", self.path)
```
output.masca_optimized:
Goal: Remove SyntaxWarning caused by unrecognized escape sequences in regex string literals in tests' webserver and clean up redundant matching.

Steps to apply (focused):
- Locate the tiny test webserver used by test requirements: test/requirements/web/webserver.py.
- Identify the Handler.do_GET method where routing is implemented. There are two occurrences of re.match("/status/(\d+)", self.path).
- Fix the SyntaxWarning by converting the regex pattern strings to raw string literals (r"/status/(\d+)").
- While editing, remove redundant double matching: perform a single match call, store the Match object, test it (if match: ...), and reuse match.group(1) rather than calling re.match twice. This both removes the warning and makes the code slightly more efficient/clear.
- Keep changes minimal and contained to Handler.do_GET so tests that import/run the script are unaffected otherwise.

Why: Python 3.12 warns about unrecognized escape sequences like "\d" in normal string literals. Using raw string literals (r"...") removes the warning and preserves the intended regex semantics. Consolidating the match avoids repeated work and is clearer.

Concrete minimal change:
- Replace both re.match("/status/(\d+)", self.path) occurrences with a single result = re.match(r"/status/(\d+)", self.path) and use if result: ... to set response_code and response_body.

files_to_modify.reason: This is the test webserver module that contains the regex pattern strings causing SyntaxWarning. The problematic re.match calls are inside Handler.do_GET and must be changed to use raw string literals and consolidated to avoid duplicate matching.

functions_to_modify.reason: do_GET contains two occurrences of re.match("/status/(\d+)", self.path). Change the pattern to a raw string (r"/status/(\d+)") to remove the SyntaxWarning and consolidate the two match calls into a single match object reuse to avoid redundant matching.

output.analysis_summary: I inspected test/requirements/web/webserver.py and found the SyntaxWarning originates from two uses of re.match("/status/(\d+)", self.path) inside Handler.do_GET. The fix is straightforward: change the pattern string to a raw string literal (r"/status/(\d+)") and, ideally, consolidate the two match calls into a single call (result = re.match(r"/status/(\d+)", self.path); if result: ...). Only test/requirements/web/webserver.py needs modification, specifically the Handler.do_GET method. This will remove the SyntaxWarning on Python 3.12+ while keeping behavior unchanged.

agents.name: context_planner_agent
agents.system_prompt:
You are a Context Planner Agent specialized in step plan generation.

## Your Task
You receive from the Analysis Agent:
- PR title and body
- Optimized MASCA
- List of files to modify
- List of functions to modify

You must:
1. **Retrieve context**: Use tools to read the context files of the identified functions
2. **Analyze dependencies**: Understand how the functions interact
3. **Generate step plan**: Create a detailed step-by-step plan

## Available Tools
- `read_context_file(file_path)`: Read a pre-generated context file
- `list_context_files(directory, pattern)`: List available context files
- `read_call_graph(section)`: Read information from the call graph

## Required Output
You must produce a `step_plan` with:
- `summary`: 1-2 sentence summary of what the PR does
- `steps`: Ordered list of steps, each with:
  - `operation`: What to do (e.g., "Add validation method")
  - `file_to_modify`: Relative path of the file to modify (e.g., "src/module.py")
  - `function_to_modify`: Name of the function to modify (e.g., "ClassName.method_name")
  - `reason`: Technical motivation
  - `side_effects`: Potential impacts

## IMPORTANT RULE for function_to_modify
- If the file to modify is a Python file (.py), specify the function/method to modify
- If the file is NOT a Python file (e.g., .js, .html, .css, .json, .yaml, .md, etc.),
  the value of `function_to_modify` MUST be null

## Guidelines for Steps
- Each step must be atomic and implementable
- The order of steps must be logical (dependencies first)
- For Python files: always specify the function in the format "FunctionName" or "ClassName.method_name"
- For non-Python files: function_to_modify must be null
- The side_effects should mention tests, dependents, and API impacts
- You MUST generate a number of step EQUAL to the number of the python functions you are intendeed to modify nomore neither less

## Step Examples

### For Python files:
```json
{
  "operation": "Add time_spanned_alpha method",
  "file_to_modify": "manimlib/animation/animation.py",
  "function_to_modify": "Animation.time_spanned_alpha",
  "reason": "To compute animation progress based on actual elapsed time",
  "side_effects": "May require downstream animations to call this method"
}
```

### For non-Python files:
```json
{
  "operation": "Update API endpoint configuration",
  "file_to_modify": "config/routes.yaml",
  "function_to_modify": null,
  "reason": "Add new route for the authentication endpoint",
  "side_effects": "Requires server restart to apply changes"
}
```
agents.input_prompt:
Generate an implementation plan for this PR:

## PR Title
fix: tests webserver syntaxwarning

## PR Body
Changed in Python version 3.12: Unrecognized escape sequences produce a SyntaxWarning. In a future Python version they will be eventually a SyntaxError.
[2.4.1.1. Escape sequences](https://docs.python.org/3/reference/lexical_analysis.html#index-23)

Change to raw string literal to fix:
```
/webserver.py:17: SyntaxWarning: invalid escape sequence '\d'
  elif re.match("/status/(\d+)", self.path):
/webserver.py:18: SyntaxWarning: invalid escape sequence '\d'
  result = re.match("/status/(\d+)", self.path)
```

## MASCA Analysis (optimized)
Goal: Remove SyntaxWarning caused by unrecognized escape sequences in regex string literals in tests' webserver and clean up redundant matching.

Steps to apply (focused):
- Locate the tiny test webserver used by test requirements: test/requirements/web/webserver.py.
- Identify the Handler.do_GET method where routing is implemented. There are two occurrences of re.match("/status/(\d+)", self.path).
- Fix the SyntaxWarning by converting the regex pattern strings to raw string literals (r"/status/(\d+)").
- While editing, remove redundant double matching: perform a single match call, store the Match object, test it (if match: ...), and reuse match.group(1) rather than calling re.match twice. This both removes the warning and makes the code slightly more efficient/clear.
- Keep changes minimal and contained to Handler.do_GET so tests that import/run the script are unaffected otherwise.

Why: Python 3.12 warns about unrecognized escape sequences like "\d" in normal string literals. Using raw string literals (r"...") removes the warning and preserves the intended regex semantics. Consolidating the match avoids repeated work and is clearer.

Concrete minimal change:
- Replace both re.match("/status/(\d+)", self.path) occurrences with a single result = re.match(r"/status/(\d+)", self.path) and use if result: ... to set response_code and response_body.

## Files to Modify
- test/requirements/web/webserver.py: This is the test webserver module that contains the regex pattern strings causing SyntaxWarning. The problematic re.match calls are inside Handler.do_GET and must be changed to use raw string literals and consolidated to avoid duplicate matching.

## Functions to Modify
- Handler.do_GET (test/requirements/web/webserver.py): do_GET contains two occurrences of re.match("/status/(\d+)", self.path). Change the pattern to a raw string (r"/status/(\d+)") to remove the SyntaxWarning and consolidate the two match calls into a single match object reuse to avoid redundant matching.

## Analysis Summary
I inspected test/requirements/web/webserver.py and found the SyntaxWarning originates from two uses of re.match("/status/(\d+)", self.path) inside Handler.do_GET. The fix is straightforward: change the pattern string to a raw string literal (r"/status/(\d+)") and, ideally, consolidate the two match calls into a single call (result = re.match(r"/status/(\d+)", self.path); if result: ...). Only test/requirements/web/webserver.py needs modification, specifically the Handler.do_GET method. This will remove the SyntaxWarning on Python 3.12+ while keeping behavior unchanged.

Use the context files to understand dependencies and generate a detailed step-by-step plan.

steps.operation: Fix regex string literals and consolidate duplicate matches in the GET handler
steps.reason: Python 3.12 issues a SyntaxWarning for unrecognized escape sequences like "\d" in normal string literals. Converting the regex pattern to a raw string (r"/status/(\d+)") removes the warning while preserving regex semantics. Consolidating the two re.match calls into one avoids redundant work and makes the control flow clearer.
steps.side_effects: Behavioral: No change to the external behavior or API of the test webserver — the same path matching and response logic is preserved. Tests: Running the test suite should no longer emit SyntaxWarning on Python 3.12+; run tests to confirm. Performance: Slight micro-optimization by avoiding a second regex match. Dependents: Any code that imports or runs this tiny test webserver is unaffected functionally; only logging/warnings are changed. Compatibility: Works the same on older Python versions; raw string preserves intended regex meaning. Suggested follow-up: run the repository tests and check for any linters or tooling that flag the change.

step_plan.summary: Replace normal regex string literals with raw string literals and consolidate duplicate re.match calls inside Handler.do_GET in test/requirements/web/webserver.py to eliminate Python 3.12 SyntaxWarning and avoid redundant matching.
